questions.label: What is real?
questions.framing: Metaphysics — the nature of being itself.
questions.statusWhy: Physics and cosmology now constrain what can be real; the realism-vs-anti-realism question stays live.

questions.label: What can I know?
questions.framing: Epistemology — knowledge, its sources and limits.
questions.statusWhy: Formal limits genuinely closed sub-questions and naive foundationalism was abandoned — but justification debates stay live.

questions.label: How does the mind work?
questions.statusWhy: Science took over large parts of the mechanics; much of higher cognition, and the hard problem, survive the handoff.

questions.label: What is the self?
questions.framing: Mind & identity — what, if anything, the 'I' is.
questions.statusWhy: A deflationary turn reframes the substantial self as a useful fiction; substance-self traditions hold the question wide open.

questions.label: How should I live?
questions.framing: Ethics — the good life and right action.
questions.statusWhy: Virtue, duty, and consequence are mature, articulate, non-converging camps.

questions.label: What is a just society?
questions.framing: Politics — power, justice, the legitimate order.
questions.statusWhy: Liberal, libertarian, communitarian, and capability views are a stable, informed disagreement.

questions.label: Is the will free?
questions.framing: Freedom & determinism — whether we author our acts.
questions.statusWhy: Compatibilist, libertarian, and hard-determinist camps persist; the underlying metaphysics resists resolution in principle.

questions.label: Why do we suffer?
questions.framing: Liberation — the root of suffering and the way to peace.
questions.statusWhy: The mechanisms of suffering were handed to medicine and psychology; read as the problem of evil, the question stays live.

questions.label: What makes life worth living?
questions.statusWhy: An evaluative, first-person residue that empirical work cannot absorb.

questions.label: How do we transform?
questions.framing: Transformation — how minds change, heal, and awaken.
questions.statusWhy: Clinical methods of change are tested but contested; the contemplative ends of transformation stay open.

questions.label: Is there an Absolute?
questions.framing: The divine, the ground of being, ultimate reality.
questions.statusWhy: Resists resolution in principle; the map takes no side.

readings.title: The Miracle of Mindfulness
readings.note: Mindfulness as practice for living and serving.

readings.author: B.R. Ambedkar
readings.title: The Buddha and His Dhamma
readings.note: Buddhism reframed for social liberation.

concepts.gloss: A mortal life wins meaning by earning undying glory; we trade length of days for a deathless name.

readings.author: Homer
readings.title: The Iliad
readings.note: Achilles and the glory that outlasts a short life.

passages.text: My good friend, if, when we were once out of this fight, we could escape old age and death thenceforward and forever, I should neither press forward myself nor bid you do so, but death in ten thousand shapes hangs ever over our heads, and no man can elude him; therefore let us go forward and either win glory for ourselves, or yield it to another.
passages.author: Homer

passages.text: My life is more to me than all the wealth of Ilius while it was yet at peace before the Achaeans went there, or than all the treasure that lies on the stone floor of Apollo’s temple beneath the cliffs of Pytho. Cattle and sheep are to be had for harrying, and a man buy both tripods and horses if he wants them, but when his life has once left him it can neither be bought nor harried back again.
passages.author: Homer
passages.locator: Book IX — Achilles to the embassy

concepts.gloss: The deepest longing is not glory but return — to home, hearth, and the self one left behind.

readings.author: Homer
readings.title: The Odyssey
readings.note: The long way home as the truer measure of a life.

concepts.gloss: A great soul falls not through vice but a single fatal error; suffering is how we come to see.

readings.author: Sophocles
readings.title: Oedipus Rex
readings.note: The fall that turns on a single blind error.

readings.author: Aristotle
readings.title: Poetics
readings.note: Where hamartia and catharsis are named.

concepts.gloss: An unwritten higher law can outrank the state's command; conscience may rightly defy power.

readings.author: Sophocles
readings.title: Antigone
readings.note: Unwritten law against the edict of the state.

concepts.gloss: Overreaching pride invites a balancing fall; the order of things corrects those who forget they are mortal.

readings.author: Aeschylus
readings.title: The Persians
readings.note: Pride overreaches and the balance falls.

readings.author: Aeschylus
readings.title: The Oresteia
readings.note: The long working-out of justice and retribution.

concepts.gloss: Seize the day; since death is certain and tomorrow unowned, gather life's joys now.

readings.author: Horace
readings.title: Odes, I.11
readings.note: 'Seize the day, trusting tomorrow as little as you can.'

concepts.gloss: To be remade you must first go down into the dark and face the dead.

readings.author: Virgil
readings.title: The Aeneid, Book VI
readings.note: The journey through the underworld and back.

concepts.gloss: The same Love that draws the soul upward moves the sun and stars; the cosmos is ordered by desire for God.

readings.author: Dante Alighieri
readings.title: The Divine Comedy: Paradiso
readings.note: 'The Love that moves the sun and the other stars.'

readings.author: Dante Alighieri
readings.title: The Divine Comedy: Inferno
readings.note: The descent that opens the whole journey.

concepts.gloss: A noble illusion can ennoble the dreamer; idealism and madness blur where the world mocks the ideal.

readings.author: Miguel de Cervantes
readings.title: Don Quixote
readings.note: The ideal that ennobles the deluded knight.

concepts.gloss: Consciousness turned upon itself can paralyse action; thinking too precisely on the event unstrings the will.

readings.author: William Shakespeare
readings.title: Hamlet
readings.note: Thought that 'unstrings' the will to act.

concepts.gloss: All the world's a stage; identity is the sequence of parts we play, not a fixed core beneath.

readings.author: William Shakespeare
readings.title: As You Like It
readings.note: 'All the world's a stage' — the seven ages of man.

concepts.gloss: Better to reign in Hell than serve in Heaven; the will to refuse can outshine obedience.

readings.author: John Milton
readings.title: Paradise Lost
readings.note: Satan's defiant 'better to reign in Hell.'

concepts.gloss: Energy and reason, good and evil are necessary contraries; without contraries there is no progression.

readings.author: William Blake
readings.title: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
readings.note: 'Without contraries is no progression.'

concepts.gloss: To seize the power to create life is to unleash what we cannot govern; the maker is undone by the made.

readings.author: Mary Shelley
readings.title: Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus
readings.note: The creator undone by what he makes.

concepts.gloss: Endless striving redeems us; whoever strives on without rest, even erring, may yet be saved.

readings.author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
readings.title: Faust, Part One
readings.note: The wager on restless, endless striving.

readings.author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
readings.title: Faust, Part Two
readings.note: Striving redeemed at the last.

concepts.gloss: The deepest knowing rests easy in uncertainty and mystery, without irritable reaching after fact and reason.

readings.author: John Keats
readings.title: Selected Letters (to George and Tom Keats, Dec. 1817)
readings.note: Dwelling 'in uncertainties, mysteries, doubts.'

concepts.gloss: Poetry is the spontaneous overflow of powerful feeling, recollected in tranquillity; imagination remakes experience.

readings.author: William Wordsworth
readings.title: Preface to Lyrical Ballads
readings.note: Poetry as feeling 'recollected in tranquillity.'

concepts.gloss: A spiteful inner voice insists we will act against our own interest just to prove we are free.

readings.author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
readings.title: Notes from Underground
readings.note: Spite that chooses against its own interest, to be free.

concepts.gloss: People flee the burden of freedom; they will trade liberty for bread, mystery, and authority.

readings.author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
readings.title: The Brothers Karamazov
readings.note: The parable of freedom traded for bread and authority.

concepts.gloss: A dying man discovers his whole respectable life was a lie — and only now begins to live.

readings.author: Leo Tolstoy
readings.title: The Death of Ivan Ilyich
readings.note: A respectable life exposed as a lie by dying.

passages.text: The syllogism he had learnt from Kiesewetter's Logic — 'Caius is a man, men are mortal, therefore Caius is mortal' — had always seemed to him correct as applied to Caius, but certainly not as applied to himself. That Caius — man in the abstract — was mortal, was perfectly correct; but he was not Caius, not an abstract man, but a creature quite, quite separate from all others.
passages.author: Leo Tolstoy

concepts.gloss: Nature wears a blank, indifferent mask; we hurl meaning at it and it stares back, unreadable.

readings.author: Herman Melville
readings.title: Moby-Dick
readings.note: The blank, indifferent mask we read for meaning.

concepts.gloss: The deep past returns whole and unbidden through a taste or scent, beyond the reach of willed recall.

readings.author: Marcel Proust
readings.title: In Search of Lost Time: Swann's Way
readings.note: The madeleine and the past returning whole.

concepts.gloss: Stripped of his use, a man becomes vermin to his own family; identity is what others can still use.

readings.author: Franz Kafka
readings.title: The Metamorphosis
readings.note: Stripped of use, the self becomes vermin to its own.

concepts.gloss: Guilt needs no crime; an unreachable authority condemns us by a law we can never read.

readings.author: Franz Kafka
readings.title: The Trial
readings.note: Condemned by a law one can never reach or read.

concepts.gloss: All possible books already exist; total knowledge and total noise become indistinguishable.

readings.author: Jorge Luis Borges
readings.title: The Library of Babel (in Ficciones)
readings.note: Every possible book, and so total noise.

concepts.gloss: Perfect, total memory destroys thought; to think at all is to forget differences and abstract.

readings.author: Jorge Luis Borges
readings.title: Funes the Memorious (in Ficciones)
readings.note: Total memory that makes thought impossible.

concepts.gloss: Tyranny by pleasure: a people pacified not by pain but by comfort, distraction, and a perfect drug.

readings.author: Aldous Huxley
readings.title: Brave New World
readings.note: A people pacified by pleasure, not pain.

concepts.gloss: Power rewrites the past and shrinks language until dissent becomes literally unthinkable.

readings.author: George Orwell
readings.title: Nineteen Eighty-Four
readings.note: Rewriting the past, shrinking language until dissent is unthinkable.

concepts.gloss: The traumatic past is not gone but a place that persists; the self must re-member what it was forced to dismember.

readings.author: Toni Morrison
readings.title: Beloved
readings.note: The traumatic past as a place that will not stay past.

concepts.gloss: A perfect city rests on one child's torment; the just refuse the bargain and simply walk away.

readings.author: Ursula K. Le Guin
readings.title: The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas
readings.note: The happiness that rests on one child's torment.

concepts.gloss: Patriarchy perfected: a theocracy that reduces women to wombs and renames the theft a sacred duty.

readings.author: Margaret Atwood
readings.title: The Handmaid's Tale
readings.note: A theocracy that reduces women to wombs.

concepts.gloss: To know your whole future is not to escape it; you live it freely and foreknown at once.

readings.author: Ted Chiang
readings.title: Story of Your Life (in Stories of Your Life and Others)
readings.note: Foreknowledge lived freely and at once.

concepts.gloss: The pathos of things: beauty is precious because it passes, and grief is the proof we loved the world.

readings.author: Murasaki Shikibu
readings.title: The Tale of Genji
readings.note: The pathos of passing things, the world's first novel.

concepts.gloss: Act for the act's own sake; do your duty wholly, yet renounce all claim upon its fruits.

readings.author: Vyasa (trad. ascription)
readings.title: The Bhagavad Gita (within the Mahabharata)
readings.note: Action without attachment to its fruits.

concepts.gloss: Thirty birds cross seven valleys to find their king — and discover the sought is the seeker itself.

readings.author: Farid ud-Din Attar
readings.title: The Conference of the Birds
readings.note: The seeker that turns out to be the sought.

concepts.gloss: The cup of fate is poured before we taste it; we are pieces moved across the board, then dropped in the box.

readings.author: Omar Khayyam
readings.title: Rubáiyát (Edward FitzGerald, trans.)
readings.note: Pieces moved across the board of fate.

concepts.gloss: The soul as the god's lover: an ecstatic, personal surrender that overrides caste, marriage, and shame.

readings.author: Mirabai
readings.title: Collected Bhajans / Padavali
readings.note: Ecstatic devotion that overrides caste and shame.

concepts.gloss: The eternal flashes in the ordinary instant — an old pond, a frog's plunge, the sound of water.

readings.author: Matsuo Bashō
readings.title: The Narrow Road to the Deep North (Oku no Hosomichi)
readings.note: The eternal flashing in the ordinary instant.

readings.author: Matsuo Bashō
readings.title: Frog Haiku
readings.note: 'Old pond — a frog leaps in, the sound of water.'

passages.text:
On a withered branch
A crow is sitting
This autumn eve.
passages.author: Matsuo Bashō
passages.locator: A History of Japanese Literature, p.295
passages.source: Aston, A History of Japanese Literature (1899)

concepts.gloss: The crippled child who cannot walk becomes king; greatness is forged against obstacles, not in their absence.

readings.author: Mande griot tradition (D. T. Niane, transcr.)
readings.title: Sundiata: An Epic of Old Mali
readings.note: The crippled child who rises to found an empire.

concepts.gloss: A lone sane man sees that the society around him is the cannibal; to save the children is to refuse inherited cruelty.

readings.author: Lu Xun
readings.title: A Madman's Diary
readings.note: Tradition seen as a society that eats its own.

concepts.gloss: Blackness reclaimed as a positive value and a shared poetic consciousness against the colonizer's gaze.

readings.author: Aimé Césaire
readings.title: Notebook of a Return to the Native Land
readings.note: Blackness reclaimed as a positive, shared value.

concepts.gloss: Every society has its own logic and dignity; colonialism shatters it not by argument but by uprooting meaning.

readings.author: Chinua Achebe
readings.title: Things Fall Apart
readings.note: A coherent African world, shattered by colonialism.

concepts.gloss: A lineage walled off from the world is doomed to repeat itself; isolation breeds the same fate over and over.

readings.author: Gabriel García Márquez
readings.title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
readings.note: A walled-off lineage doomed to repeat its fate.

concepts.gloss: One is not born, but becomes, a woman; she is cast as man's Other, never the absolute subject.

readings.author: Simone de Beauvoir
readings.title: The Second Sex
readings.note: 'One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.'

concepts.gloss: A person is a person through other persons; the self is woven from community, not prior to it.

readings.author: Mogobe B. Ramose
readings.title: African Philosophy Through Ubuntu
readings.note: The systematic statement: being is be-ing-with, the self through others.

readings.author: Kwame Gyekye
readings.title: An Essay on African Philosophical Thought
readings.note: Akan personhood — communal yet not swallowing the individual.

concepts.gloss: A vital force runs through word and world — the power that makes things happen, alive in speech, breath, and being.

readings.author: Wándé Abímbólá
readings.title: Ifá: An Exposition of Ifá Literary Corpus
readings.note: An initiated babaláwo and scholar opens the Yoruba corpus from within.

readings.author: Wándé Abímbólá
readings.title: Ifá Will Mend Our Broken World
readings.note: Àṣẹ and Yoruba thought addressed to a global readership.

concepts.gloss: All beings see themselves as persons; what differs across the living world is not culture but the body through which it is seen.

readings.author: Eduardo Viveiros de Castro
readings.title: Cosmological Deixis and Amerindian Perspectivism
readings.note: The essay that named multinaturalism: one culture, many natures.

readings.author: Eduardo Viveiros de Castro
readings.title: Cannibal Metaphysics
readings.note: The fuller working-out of the Amerindian theory of the world.

concepts.gloss: The holy is bound to place, not time; revelation rises from particular land, not from a line of history.

readings.author: Vine Deloria Jr.
readings.title: God Is Red: A Native View of Religion
readings.note: Space over time: religion rooted in sacred place, not sacred history.

readings.author: Kyle Whyte
readings.title: Indigenous Climate Change Studies: Indigenizing Futures
readings.note: Land-based responsibilities carried forward to descendants.

concepts.gloss: The land is the law and the ground of identity; to be a person is to hold a custodial relationship with country.

readings.author: Mary Graham
readings.title: Some Thoughts about the Philosophical Underpinnings of Aboriginal Worldviews
readings.note: The two precepts: the land is the law, and you are not alone in the world.

concepts.gloss: Do the moral feelings spring from principle and the raw passions from matter? Korea's central argument on the sources of the heart.

readings.author: Yi Hwang (Toegye)
readings.title: Ten Diagrams on Sage Learning (Seonghak sipdo)
readings.note: The mature statement of li-primacy in moral psychology.

readings.author: Yi Hwang & Gi Daeseung
readings.title: The Four-Seven Correspondence
readings.note: The eight-year exchange of letters that framed the debate.

concepts.gloss: Principle never moves on its own; it rides on the material force that alone acts — the two forever inseparable.

readings.author: Yi I (Yulgok)
readings.title: Essentials of Sage Learning (Seonghak jibyo)
readings.note: Principle and material force as one: qi acts, li rides.

concepts.gloss: Each school grasps a corner of the truth; wisdom harmonizes their quarrels back into one mind.

readings.author: Wŏnhyo
readings.title: Commentary on the Awakening of Mahāyāna Faith (Daeseung gisillon so)
readings.note: The 'one mind' that reconciles the doctrinal schools.

concepts.gloss: Awakening breaks open in an instant, but old habits wear away only slowly; insight and practice complete each other.

readings.author: Jinul
readings.title: Secrets on Cultivating the Mind (Susimgyeol)
readings.note: Sudden insight followed by gradual practice — Seon and doctrine joined.

concepts.gloss: Every person bears Heaven within; to serve another human being is to serve the divine — and all are equal in it.

readings.author: Choe Je-u
readings.title: Eastern Scripture (Donggyeong daejeon)
readings.note: The founding text of Donghak: Heaven borne within every person.

concepts.gloss: Love as self-giving gift, not desire for the lovable — patient, unearned, poured out even on the enemy.

readings.author: Paul of Tarsus
readings.title: First Epistle to the Corinthians (ch. 13)
readings.note: 'Love is patient, love is kind' — agapē as self-giving love.

readings.author: Anders Nygren
readings.title: Agape and Eros
readings.note: The classic contrast of self-giving agapē with acquisitive eros.

concepts.gloss: Salvation is an unearned gift, not a wage; no ladder of works reaches it — only the gift descends.

readings.author: Augustine of Hippo
readings.title: On Grace and Free Will
readings.note: Salvation as unmerited gift, not earned by works.

readings.author: Augustine of Hippo
readings.title: Confessions
readings.note: The lived drama of grace and a will divided against itself.

concepts.gloss: Human nature is born already fractured; the will is divided against itself and cannot heal itself alone.

readings.author: Augustine of Hippo
readings.title: On the Merits and Forgiveness of Sins
readings.note: The inherited fault that the will cannot undo on its own.

readings.author: Book of Genesis
readings.title: Genesis 3
readings.note: The Fall in the garden — the source narrative.

concepts.gloss: In the beginning was the Word — the divine reason through which all things are made and hold together.

readings.author: Gospel of John
readings.title: John 1:1-14
readings.note: 'In the beginning was the Word' — the Logos through whom all is made.

readings.author: Philo of Alexandria
readings.title: On the Creation of the World
readings.note: The Logos as God's mediating reason, bridging Greek and Hebrew thought.

concepts.gloss: A binding pact between God and a people: chosen and obligated at once, sealed by law.

readings.author: Hebrew Bible
readings.title: Exodus & Deuteronomy
readings.note: The binding pact between God and a people, with law as its seal.

readings.author: Jon D. Levenson
readings.title: Sinai and Zion
readings.note: The covenant as the spine of Hebrew religion.

concepts.gloss: Every human is made in the image of God, and so bears an inviolable worth no station can erase.

readings.author: Book of Genesis
readings.title: Genesis 1:26-27
readings.note: Humankind made 'in the image of God.'

readings.author: Maimonides
readings.title: The Guide for the Perplexed
readings.note: The image read as the intellect, not the body.

concepts.gloss: Creation shattered and the divine sparks scattered; our task is to gather them — to repair a broken world.

readings.author: Isaac Luria (Hayyim Vital, recorder)
readings.title: Etz Hayim (The Tree of Life)
readings.note: Gathering the scattered divine sparks — repairing a broken world.

concepts.gloss: God is absolutely one — no partner, no division, no likeness; all multiplicity hangs from a single source.

readings.author: The Quran
readings.title: Surah al-Ikhlas (112)
readings.note: 'Say: He is God, the One' — absolute divine unity.

readings.author: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari
readings.title: Kitab al-Luma
readings.note: The Ash'arite formulation of God's unity and attributes.

concepts.gloss: Every soul is born with an innate disposition toward truth and the divine, before culture overlays it.

readings.author: The Quran
readings.title: Surah ar-Rum (30:30)
readings.note: Every soul born upon the fitra — an innate disposition to the truth.

readings.author: al-Ghazali
readings.title: Deliverance from Error
readings.note: The innate nature beneath acquired belief.

concepts.gloss: The cosmos is a battlefield of truth against the lie, light against dark; each soul must choose a side, and the choice matters.

readings.author: Zarathustra
readings.title: The Gathas (Yasna 30 & 45)
readings.note: The cosmic choice between truth (asha) and the lie (druj).

readings.author: Mary Boyce
readings.title: Zoroastrians: Their Religious Beliefs and Practices
readings.note: The standard scholarly account of the tradition.

concepts.gloss: On a fleeting, slippery earth, truth cannot be grasped directly — only flower-and-song, poetry and art, can speak it.

readings.author: Nezahualcoyotl & the tlamatinime
readings.title: Cantares Mexicanos
readings.note: Flower-and-song: art as the only truth on the slippery earth.

readings.author: Miguel León-Portilla
readings.title: Aztec Thought and Culture
readings.note: Reconstructs Nahua philosophy from the Nahuatl sources.

concepts.gloss: A king storms the world for deathlessness, loses it, and learns the only immortality is the work and the city he leaves behind.

readings.author: Sîn-lēqi-unninni (standard version)
readings.title: The Epic of Gilgamesh
readings.note: The king who seeks deathlessness and learns to accept mortality.

concepts.gloss: Truth, justice, and cosmic order are one principle; the king upholds ma'at against chaos, and each heart is weighed by it.

readings.author: Ptahhotep
readings.title: The Maxims of Ptahhotep
readings.note: Living by ma'at — truth, order, and justice as one principle.

readings.author: Egyptian tradition
readings.title: The Book of the Dead (ch. 125)
readings.note: The heart weighed against the feather of ma'at.

concepts.gloss: Knowledge begins not in doubt but in wonder; awe before the sheer existence of things is the root of insight and the religious life.

readings.author: Abraham Joshua Heschel
readings.title: God in Search of Man
readings.note: 'Radical amazement' — wonder, not doubt, as the root of knowing.

readings.author: Abraham Joshua Heschel
readings.title: The Sabbath
readings.note: Time made holy — a cathedral built in hours, not in stone.

concepts.gloss: The cosmos mirrors itself at every scale; the human being is a small world bound by hidden correspondence to the great one.

readings.author: Hermes Trismegistus (attrib.)
readings.title: The Emerald Tablet
readings.note: 'That which is below is like that which is above' — the law of correspondence.

readings.author: Corpus Hermeticum
readings.title: Poimandres & the Hermetic treatises
readings.note: The Greco-Egyptian source of Hermetic cosmology.

concepts.gloss: A spark of the divine lies trapped in matter; we are asleep in a flawed world, and salvation comes not by faith but by awakening secret knowledge.

readings.author: Valentinus / Nag Hammadi
readings.title: The Gospel of Truth
readings.note: The divine spark, the sleep of the world, and awakening through gnosis.

readings.author: Hans Jonas
readings.title: The Gnostic Religion
readings.note: The classic modern reconstruction of Gnostic thought.

concepts.gloss: Turning base metal to gold is a mask for the real work: the slow transmutation of the soul through dissolution and rebirth — solve et coagula.

readings.author: Zosimos of Panopolis
readings.title: The Visions of Zosimos
readings.note: Alchemical transformation read as the soul's death and rebirth.

readings.author: C. G. Jung
readings.title: Psychology and Alchemy
readings.note: Alchemy decoded as a projection of inner individuation.

concepts.gloss: Beneath every religion's surface lies one and the same mystical truth; the traditions are many dialects of a single perennial wisdom.

readings.author: Agostino Steuco
readings.title: De perenni philosophia
readings.note: The coinage: a single wisdom recurring across all ages.

readings.author: Aldous Huxley
readings.title: The Perennial Philosophy
readings.note: The 20th-century statement that all mystics meet at one summit.

concepts.gloss: Even the gods are mortal; the world ends in fire and a green world rises after — meaning is the courage to meet a doom that fate has already fixed.

readings.author: Snorri Sturluson
readings.title: The Prose Edda
readings.note: The doom of the gods — and the green world that rises after.

readings.author: Anonymous
readings.title: The Poetic Edda: Völuspá
readings.note: The seeress's prophecy of the world's end and renewal.

concepts.gloss: Turn the light of awareness back upon its source and let it circulate; the spirit, no longer spent outward, coagulates into an immortal self — the golden flower.

readings.author: Lü Dongbin tradition
readings.title: The Secret of the Golden Flower (太乙金華宗旨)
readings.note: Inner alchemy: circulating the light to coagulate the immortal spirit.

readings.author: Richard Wilhelm (trans.), C. G. Jung (comm.)
readings.title: The Secret of the Golden Flower
readings.note: The translation that carried Daoist neidan — and Jung's commentary — to the West.

concepts.gloss: Still the mind, draw it down into the heart, and pray the Name without ceasing until the uncreated light dawns within.

readings.author: Gregory Palamas
readings.title: The Triads (In Defence of the Holy Hesychasts)
readings.note: The theological defence of the bodily prayer of the heart and the vision of the uncreated light.

readings.author: Various (comp. Nikodimos & Makarios)
readings.title: The Philokalia
readings.note: The anthology that gathered the hesychast masters and carried the Jesus Prayer down to the present.

concepts.gloss: A graded month of imaginative meditation and daily self-examination, training the soul to discern the spirits and choose freely for the good.

readings.author: Ignatius of Loyola
readings.title: The Spiritual Exercises
readings.note: The director's manual for a four-week graded retreat of imaginative meditation and discernment.

readings.author: George E. Ganss (trans.)
readings.title: The Spiritual Exercises of Saint Ignatius: A Translation and Commentary
readings.note: A modern annotated rendering that makes the terse 16th-century rubrics usable.

concepts.gloss: Awareness is already pure and perfect; nothing need be added or removed — simply recognize the naked, self-knowing ground and rest there.

readings.author: Longchenpa
readings.title: The Precious Treasury of the Basic Space of Phenomena (Chöying Dzö)
readings.note: The systematic exposition of the natural great perfection by its supreme scholar-yogin.

readings.author: Padmasambhava (attrib.); W. Y. Evans-Wentz (ed.)
readings.title: The Tibetan Book of the Great Liberation
readings.note: An early Western window onto the doctrine of self-liberation through recognizing the ground.

concepts.gloss: Watch the body, feelings, mind, and phenomena arise and pass; in bare, sustained attention, see directly how all things are impermanent and not-self.

readings.author: Pali Canon
readings.title: Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta (Majjhima Nikāya 10)
readings.note: The discourse that lays out the four foundations — body, feelings, mind, phenomena — as the direct path.

readings.author: Nyanaponika Thera
readings.title: The Heart of Buddhist Meditation
readings.note: The book that brought satipaṭṭhāna practice to a modern readership and seeded the mindfulness movement.

concepts.gloss: Repeat the divine Name with breath and heart until rememberer, remembered, and remembrance become one and the heart is polished to a mirror.

readings.author: Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh al-Iskandarī
readings.title: The Key to Salvation (Miftāḥ al-Falāḥ)
readings.note: A Shadhili master's manual of the methods, manners, and fruits of the invocation of the divine Name.

readings.author: Abū Ḥāmid al-Ghazālī
readings.title: The Revival of the Religious Sciences (Iḥyāʾ ʿulūm al-dīn)
readings.note: The great synthesis whose books on invocation and the remembrance of God frame the practice.

concepts.gloss: Justified true belief can still fail to be knowledge: you can be right for the wrong reasons, by luck.

readings.author: Edmund Gettier
readings.title: Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?
readings.note: The three-page paper that broke the classical definition of knowledge.

readings.author: Linda Zagzebski
readings.title: The Inescapability of Gettier Problems
readings.note: Shows why luck defeats any justification-plus-truth analysis.

concepts.gloss: No number of past observations logically guarantees the future; the sunrise we expect rests on habit, not proof.

readings.author: David Hume
readings.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
readings.note: The original argument that induction has no rational, only customary, ground.

readings.author: Nelson Goodman
readings.title: Fact, Fiction, and Forecast
readings.note: The 'new riddle' (grue) that deepened Hume's problem.

concepts.gloss: If God is all-good and all-powerful, why is there suffering? The oldest argument against a benevolent Absolute.

readings.author: David Hume
readings.title: Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
readings.note: Philo's relentless statement of the evidential problem of evil.

readings.author: J. L. Mackie
readings.title: Evil and Omnipotence
readings.note: The 20th-century formulation of the logical problem of evil.

concepts.gloss: Freedom-from (no one coercing me) versus freedom-to (mastering myself) — and the warning that 'positive' liberty can license tyranny.

readings.author: Isaiah Berlin
readings.title: Two Concepts of Liberty
readings.note: The inaugural lecture distinguishing negative from positive liberty.

readings.author: Isaiah Berlin
readings.title: Four Essays on Liberty
readings.note: The collection that set the essay in its wider argument.

concepts.gloss: The only ground for coercing anyone against their will is to prevent harm to others; over himself, the individual is sovereign.

readings.author: John Stuart Mill
readings.title: On Liberty
readings.note: The classic defence of individual liberty against social and state coercion.

concepts.gloss: Justice is in how holdings arose — just acquisition and transfer — not in any end-pattern; the minimal state is the most that's justified.

readings.author: Robert Nozick
readings.title: Anarchy, State, and Utopia
readings.note: The libertarian reply to Rawls: justice in holdings, and the minimal state.

concepts.gloss: To weigh a being's suffering less because it is not human is speciesism — the same error as racism or sexism.

readings.author: Peter Singer
readings.title: Animal Liberation
readings.note: The book that launched the modern animal-rights movement on utilitarian grounds.

concepts.gloss: Rational capitalism, once a calling, hardens into a cage of bureaucratic order that disenchants the world and outlasts its faith.

readings.author: Max Weber
readings.title: The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism
readings.note: Traces rational capitalism to Calvinist asceticism — and to the cage it became.

readings.author: Talcott Parsons (trans.)
readings.title: The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism
readings.note: The translation that carried 'the iron cage' into English.

concepts.gloss: To be Black in white America is to see yourself always through others' eyes — a twoness, two warring selves in one body.

readings.author: W. E. B. Du Bois
readings.title: The Souls of Black Folk
readings.note: The founding text of African-American philosophy and the idea of double consciousness.

concepts.gloss: There is no clean line between truths-by-meaning and truths-by-fact; belief faces experience as a whole web, revisable anywhere.

readings.author: W. V. O. Quine
readings.title: Two Dogmas of Empiricism
readings.note: The paper that dismantled the analytic/synthetic distinction and reductionism.

readings.author: W. V. O. Quine
readings.title: From a Logical Point of View
readings.note: The essay collection in which the paper appeared and was elaborated.

concepts.gloss: Stop asking whether machines 'think'; if a machine's conversation is indistinguishable from a human's, that is enough.

readings.author: Alan Turing
readings.title: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
readings.note: Reframes 'can machines think?' as the imitation game.

concepts.gloss: Mary knows every physical fact about color yet learns something new on first seeing red — so not all facts are physical.

readings.author: Frank Jackson
readings.title: Epiphenomenal Qualia
readings.note: Introduces Mary the colour scientist against physicalism.

readings.author: Frank Jackson
readings.title: What Mary Didn't Know
readings.note: The tighter restatement of the knowledge argument.

concepts.gloss: Suffering is not only borne inwardly but structural injustice to be fought; God takes a preferential option for the poor.

readings.author: Gustavo Gutiérrez
readings.title: A Theology of Liberation
readings.note: The founding work: salvation as liberation and the preferential option for the poor.

readings.author: Leonardo Boff & Clodovis Boff
readings.title: Introducing Liberation Theology
readings.note: A concise overview of the movement's method and claims.

concepts.gloss: There is one formless, timeless reality whose name is truth; it pervades all and is reached by remembering it — not by ritual, priest, or caste.

readings.author: Guru Nanak
readings.title: Japji Sahib (in the Ādi Granth / Guru Granth Sahib)
readings.note: The opening Mūl Mantar — 'Ik Onkar' — that begins the Sikh scripture.

readings.author: Eleanor Nesbitt
readings.title: Sikhism: A Very Short Introduction
readings.note: A concise scholarly entry into Sikh belief and practice.

concepts.gloss: Service to others, offered with no thought of reward, is worship made real — and the great leveller that dissolves caste and rank.

readings.author: Guru Nanak and the Sikh Gurus
readings.title: The Guru Granth Sahib
readings.note: The scripture whose hymns make sevā and the equality of all the heart of Sikh life.

readings.author: W. H. McLeod
readings.title: Sikhism
readings.note: A standard historical-critical overview of the tradition and its ethics.

concepts.gloss: Reality is two: puruṣa, pure witnessing consciousness, and prakṛti, ever-active nature; liberation is learning to tell them apart.

readings.author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
readings.title: Sāṃkhya-kārikā
readings.note: The classic seventy verses laying out the dualism of puruṣa and prakṛti.

readings.author: Gerald James Larson
readings.title: Classical Sāṃkhya
readings.note: The standard modern study and translation of the system.

concepts.gloss: The self is not to be dissolved but strengthened; the ego, tempered and made vital, becomes God's free co-worker — not a drop lost in the sea.

readings.author: Muhammad Iqbal
readings.title: Asrār-i Khudī (The Secrets of the Self)
readings.note: The Persian poem arguing for the strengthening, not the dissolving, of the self.

readings.author: R. A. Nicholson (trans.)
readings.title: The Secrets of the Self
readings.note: The translation that carried Iqbal's doctrine of khudī to English readers.

readings.author: Muhammad Iqbal
readings.title: The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam
readings.note: His philosophical prose statement of the dynamic, creative ego.

concepts.gloss: A name carries both a reference — what it picks out — and a sense, the way it is presented; 'morning star' and 'evening star' name one planet under two senses.

readings.author: Gottlob Frege
readings.title: Über Sinn und Bedeutung (On Sense and Reference)
readings.note: The paper that split a name's reference from its sense.

readings.author: Michael Dummett
readings.title: Frege: Philosophy of Language
readings.note: The landmark reconstruction of Frege's philosophy of language.

concepts.gloss: 'The present King of France' is a disguised quantifier, not a name; descriptions melt into logical form, so a sentence can name nothing and come out false rather than nonsense.

readings.author: Bertrand Russell
readings.title: On Denoting
readings.note: The essay in Mind introducing the theory of descriptions.

readings.author: Stephen Neale
readings.title: Descriptions
readings.note: The modern defence and development of Russell's analysis.

concepts.gloss: Names are rigid designators, fixed by a causal chain of use rather than a description — so some truths are necessary yet known only after looking.

readings.author: Saul Kripke
readings.title: Naming and Necessity
readings.note: The three lectures on rigid designation and the necessary a posteriori.

readings.author: Scott Soames
readings.title: Beyond Rigidity
readings.note: A leading account of what Kripke's argument does and does not establish.

concepts.id: How to Do Things with Words
concepts.gloss: To say something is often to do something — 'I promise', 'I name this ship'; much of language performs acts rather than describing facts.

readings.author: J. L. Austin
readings.title: How to Do Things with Words
readings.note: The William James Lectures that founded speech-act theory.

readings.author: John R. Searle
readings.title: Speech Acts
readings.note: The systematic theory built directly on Austin's lectures.

concepts.gloss: What a speaker means outruns what the words say; talk runs on shared maxims, so we convey more by implying than by stating.

readings.author: Paul Grice
readings.title: Logic and Conversation
readings.note: The lectures introducing implicature and the maxims of conversation.

readings.author: Stephen Neale
readings.title: Paul Grice and the Philosophy of Language
readings.note: A clear survey of Grice's program, in Linguistics and Philosophy.

concepts.gloss: The bond between word and concept is arbitrary; each sign takes its value only from its difference from the others in the system.

readings.author: Ferdinand de Saussure
readings.title: Course in General Linguistics
readings.note: The posthumous lectures that founded structural linguistics.

readings.author: Jonathan Culler
readings.title: Ferdinand de Saussure
readings.note: A concise critical introduction to Saussure's thought.

concepts.gloss: Consciousness is no soul but a quality that arises when the four elements combine — as the power to intoxicate arises when ferments are mixed — and ends when the body does.

readings.author: Ramkrishna Bhattacharya
readings.title: Studies on the Cārvāka/Lokāyata
readings.note: The school's own texts are lost; this reconstructs them from the hostile quotations of its rivals — the standard modern study.

readings.author: Debiprasad Chattopadhyaya
readings.title: Lokāyata: A Study in Ancient Indian Materialism
readings.note: The seminal reconstruction of India's materialist current.

concepts.gloss: Right action is tikanga — correct practice rooted in values like mana, tapu and manaakitanga; ethics is keeping relationships in balance, not obeying an abstract rule.

readings.author: Hirini Moko Mead
readings.title: Tikanga Māori: Living by Māori Values
readings.note: A Māori scholar's systematic account of tikanga — a modern codification of a tradition long carried in practice, not text.

readings.author: Cleve Barlow
readings.title: Tikanga Whakaaro: Key Concepts in Māori Culture
readings.note: A bilingual lexicon of the core values tikanga rests on.

concepts.gloss: Reality is one woven fabric, each thing carrying mauri, a binding life-force; cosmos and person are continuous, not split into mind and matter or subject and object.

readings.author: Māori Marsden
readings.title: The Woven Universe: Selected Writings
readings.note: Marsden's essays articulating a Māori cosmology; the dated text codifies an older oral tradition. Ed. Te Ahukaramū Charles Royal.

readings.author: Manuka Henare
readings.title: Tapu, Mana, Mauri, Hau, Wairua: A Māori Philosophy of Vitalism and Cosmos
readings.note: A Māori scholar's synthesis of the core vitalist concepts.

concepts.gloss: To live well is to keep ayni — the binding reciprocity by which people, community and the living world give and return in balance; obligation, not contract, holds the world together.

readings.author: Fernando Huanacuni Mamani
readings.title: Vivir Bien / Buen Vivir: Filosofía, políticas, estrategias
readings.note: An Aymara author on Andean reciprocity and balance.

readings.author: Josef Estermann
readings.title: Filosofía Andina
readings.note: A systematic reconstruction of Andean thought as a relational philosophy of reciprocity.

readings.author: Catherine J. Allen
readings.title: The Hold Life Has: Coca and Cultural Identity in an Andean Community
readings.note: An ethnography grounding ayni in lived Quechua practice.

concepts.gloss: Opposites — male and female, day and night — are not rivals to be resolved but yanantin: paired halves that complete each other; reality runs on balanced, productive tension.

readings.author: Tristan Platt
readings.title: Mirrors and Maize: The Concept of Yanantin among the Macha of Bolivia
readings.note: The foundational study of Andean complementary opposition.

readings.author: Hillary S. Webb
readings.title: Yanantin and Masintin in the Andean World
readings.note: Complementary dualism in contemporary Peru, drawn from interviews and fieldwork.

concepts.gloss: Socrates' trap: is an act good because the gods command it, or do they command it because it is already good? Either morality is an arbitrary divine whim, or its standard stands above the gods — and needs no god to ground it.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Euthyphro (trans. G.M.A. Grube, in Plato: Complete Works, ed. Cooper)
readings.note: The dialogue itself; Socrates presses the question on the seer Euthyphro at the courthouse steps.

readings.author: Plato
readings.title: Euthyphro, Apology, Crito, Phaedo (trans. H.N. Fowler, Loeb)
readings.note: Greek-facing scholarly edition of the trial dialogues.

readings.author: Mark Murphy
readings.title: Theological Voluntarism (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
readings.note: Maps the dilemma onto modern divine-command ethics and its replies.

concepts.gloss: Hume's guillotine: from premises about what *is* the case, no conclusion about what *ought* to be can validly follow. Fact and value are split by a gap that no argument from nature alone can cross.

readings.author: David Hume
readings.title: A Treatise of Human Nature
readings.note: Book III.i.1 — the famous turn from 'is' and 'is not' to 'ought' and 'ought not'.

readings.author: W.D. Hudson (ed.)
readings.title: The Is/Ought Question
readings.note: The classic anthology that named and debated 'Hume's Law'.

concepts.gloss: Our moral talk reaches for objective values woven into the world — duties that would bind us whatever we felt. There are no such queer facts, so every positive moral claim is, strictly, false.

readings.author: J.L. Mackie
readings.title: Ethics: Inventing Right and Wrong
readings.note: The arguments from relativity and from queerness against objective values.

readings.author: Richard Joyce
readings.title: The Myth of Morality
readings.note: Defends and extends error theory toward moral fictionalism.

concepts.gloss: Whatever natural fact you equate with goodness — pleasure, survival, what we desire — it stays sensible to ask 'but is that good?' So 'good' names a simple, non-natural property, and defining it by nature is a fallacy.

readings.author: G.E. Moore
readings.title: Principia Ethica
readings.note: 'Good' as a simple, indefinable non-natural property; the naturalistic fallacy.

readings.author: W.D. Ross
readings.title: The Right and the Good
readings.note: A non-naturalist companion: self-evident prima facie duties.

readings.author: Thomas Baldwin
readings.title: G.E. Moore
readings.note: The standard critical study of Moore, including the open-question argument's fate.

concepts.gloss: Moral words state no facts; they vent and stir feeling. 'Stealing is wrong' adds nothing factual to 'stealing' — it means roughly 'Boo, stealing!' and tries to move you to feel the same.

readings.author: A.J. Ayer
readings.title: Language, Truth and Logic
readings.note: Chapter 6: moral judgements are not statements but expressions of feeling.

readings.author: C.L. Stevenson
readings.title: Ethics and Language
readings.note: Systematic emotivism — the persuasive, dynamic use of moral words.

readings.author: Simon Blackburn
readings.title: Ruling Passions
readings.note: Quasi-realism: how an expressivist can earn back the talk of moral truth.

concepts.gloss: Duty (dharma) is a future-facing 'ought' no perception or inference can reach; it is known only through the Veda's injunctions — an authorless, self-validating Word that needs no God or author to vouch for it.

readings.author: Jaimini
readings.title: The Pūrva Mīmāṃsā Sūtras of Jaimini (trans. Ganganatha Jha)
readings.note: The root sūtras: dharma is the object enjoined by the Vedic injunction (codanā).

readings.author: Francis X. Clooney
readings.title: Thinking Ritually: Rediscovering the Pūrva Mīmāṃsā of Jaimini
readings.note: The leading study of Jaimini's system of ritual obligation and the authority of the Word.

readings.author: John A. Taber
readings.title: Kumārila (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
readings.note: On the intrinsic validity (svataḥ prāmāṇya) of the authorless Veda.

concepts.gloss: Are good and evil (ḥusn wa qubḥ) qualities reason can discover for itself, as the Muʿtazila held — or is a thing good only because God commands it, as the Ashʿarīs replied? Islam's own Euthyphro.

readings.author: George F. Hourani
readings.title: Islamic Rationalism: The Ethics of ʿAbd al-Jabbār
readings.note: The standard study of Muʿtazilite moral rationalism — good and evil knowable by reason.

readings.author: George F. Hourani
readings.title: Reason and Tradition in Islamic Ethics
readings.note: Essays mapping the rationalist (Muʿtazila) and voluntarist (Ashʿarī) camps.

readings.author: Majid Fakhry
readings.title: Ethical Theories in Islam
readings.note: Surveys the kalām divide over the source of moral value.

concepts.gloss: The mind just is the brain: each mental state is identical to a brain state, the way lightning turned out to be electrical discharge — no extra mental stuff, no soul, feeling pain simply is a certain neural firing. (The standard reply: one mind-kind could run on many different physical systems, so perhaps only each particular state, not each type, is physical.)

readings.author: U.T. Place
readings.title: Is Consciousness a Brain Process? (British Journal of Psychology)
readings.note: The founding paper: consciousness as a brain process, distinguishing the 'is' of identity from the 'is' of predication.

readings.author: J.J.C. Smart
readings.title: Sensations and Brain Processes (Philosophical Review)
readings.note: The classic statement of type-identity; answers the standard objections to 'sensations are brain processes'.

readings.author: Cynthia Macdonald
readings.title: Mind-Body Identity Theories
readings.note: A book-length critical reconstruction of identity theory and the multiple-realizability challenge.

concepts.gloss: Every mental event is some physical event — yet no strict laws link the mental to the physical. Mind is neither a separate substance nor reducible to neuroscience: the very same events, caught in two vocabularies that never lock together into law.

readings.author: Donald Davidson
readings.title: Mental Events
readings.note: The founding essay: token-identity with no strict psychophysical laws, reconciling causation, anomalism, and monism.

readings.author: Donald Davidson
readings.title: Essays on Actions and Events
readings.note: Collects 'Mental Events' with the action-theory papers that frame the view.

readings.author: Jaegwon Kim
readings.title: Psychophysical Laws (in Actions and Events, ed. LePore & McLaughlin)
readings.note: The influential epiphenomenalism objection — does anomalous monism leave the mental causally idle?

concepts.gloss: 'Belief,' 'desire,' 'pain' belong to folk psychology — a primitive theory of mind that, like phlogiston or the four humours, a mature neuroscience may show to be simply false. The bet is that these everyday categories will be replaced, not tidily reduced.

readings.author: Paul M. Churchland
readings.title: Eliminative Materialism and the Propositional Attitudes (Journal of Philosophy)
readings.note: The manifesto: folk psychology as a false and stagnant empirical theory due for replacement.

readings.author: Stephen Stich
readings.title: From Folk Psychology to Cognitive Science
readings.note: An independent argument against the belief-desire framework from the philosophy of cognitive science.

readings.author: Patricia S. Churchland
readings.title: Neurophilosophy: Toward a Unified Science of the Mind-Brain
readings.note: The neuroscientific case for reduction/elimination (leans reductive).

concepts.gloss: All we ever meet is consciousness and its images; the seeming split between an inner grasper and an outer grasped world is itself constructed by mind. Not that nothing exists — but that experience is 'mind-only' (vijñapti-mātra), structured through and through by consciousness.

readings.author: Vasubandhu
readings.title: Seven Works of Vasubandhu (trans. Stefan Anacker)
readings.note: Includes the Viṃśatikā and Triṃśikā — the verses on vijñapti-mātra ('mind-only').

readings.author: Thomas A. Kochumuttom
readings.title: A Buddhist Doctrine of Experience
readings.note: Reads Vasubandhu as a realistic pluralist about experience, not a denier of the world.

readings.author: Dan Lusthaus
readings.title: Buddhist Phenomenology
readings.note: Argues Yogācāra is a phenomenology of consciousness, correcting the 'idealism = illusion' misreading.

concepts.gloss: We are born with four moral 'sprouts' (duan): the pang of compassion, the sense of shame, the impulse to defer, the feel for right and wrong. Goodness is these sprouts grown to maturity — human nature tends to the good as water tends downhill, and cruelty is a stunting, not our origin.

readings.author: Mencius (comp. disciples)
readings.title: Mengzi (trans. Bryan W. Van Norden, with traditional commentaries)
readings.note: The four-sprouts argument (2A6, 6A); the now-standard scholarly English.

readings.author: Mencius
readings.title: Mencius (trans. D.C. Lau, Penguin Classics)
readings.note: The older standard rendering of the Mengzi.

readings.author: Kwong-loi Shun
readings.title: Mencius and Early Chinese Thought
readings.note: The leading philosophical reconstruction of the xing-shan (good-nature) thesis.

concepts.gloss: Left to itself, human nature is crooked (è) — spontaneously grasping, partial, prone to strife. What we call virtue is a deliberate human craft (wei), straightened into us by ritual, teachers and the sages, the way a warped board is steamed flat. Not innate evil — raw material that needs working.

readings.author: Xunzi
readings.title: Xunzi: The Complete Text (trans. Eric L. Hutton)
readings.note: Includes the chapter 'Human Nature Is Bad' (性惡); now the scholarly default.

readings.author: Xunzi
readings.title: Xunzi: A Translation and Study of the Complete Works (trans. John Knoblock)
readings.note: The fuller three-volume apparatus and commentary.

readings.author: Paul R. Goldin
readings.title: Rituals of the Way: The Philosophy of Xunzi
readings.note: On virtue as deliberate artifice (wei) and the reshaping power of ritual (li).

concepts.gloss: Moral understanding is won from the outside in: investigate the principle (li) inherent in each thing and affair, one after another, until one day it breaks through into comprehensive insight. Knowledge precedes and guides right action — the gradual, studious road to sagehood.

readings.author: Zhu Xi
readings.title: Learning to Be a Sage (trans. Daniel K. Gardner)
readings.note: Selections from the Zhuzi yulei on gewu — investigating the principle (li) in things.

readings.author: Wing-tsit Chan
readings.title: A Source Book in Chinese Philosophy
readings.note: Chapter 34 gathers the core Zhu Xi texts on the extension of knowledge.

readings.author: Hoyt Cleveland Tillman
readings.title: Confucian Discourse and Chu Hsi's Ascendancy
readings.note: How the Cheng-Zhu programme of inquiry became Neo-Confucian orthodoxy.

concepts.gloss: Time is not a road things travel down; each existent is time and each time is an existent. The pine is time, the moment is the whole of being. Time does not flow past us — it is what we are, total and complete in every now.

readings.author: Dōgen
readings.title: Treasury of the True Dharma Eye: Shōbōgenzō (ed. Kazuaki Tanahashi)
readings.note: The complete Shōbōgenzō; the 'Uji' (Being-Time) fascicle is the source.

readings.author: Dōgen
readings.title: The Heart of Dōgen's Shōbōgenzō (trans. Norman Waddell & Masao Abe)
readings.note: Key fascicles with introduction; a close rendering of 'Uji'.

readings.author: Hee-Jin Kim
readings.title: Eihei Dōgen: Mystical Realist
readings.note: The standard study; reads Uji as a metaphysics of time, not a mystical figure.

concepts.gloss: What looks like cause and effect is only God's habit: fire does not burn the cotton — God creates the burning when they touch, and could withhold it. 'Necessary connection' is our expectation, not a power in things; only God truly acts. (His quarrel with the philosophers was over divine freedom and miracle, not a ban on inquiry.)

readings.author: al-Ghazālī
readings.title: The Incoherence of the Philosophers (Tahāfut al-Falāsifa, trans. Michael E. Marmura)
readings.note: The 17th Discussion: there is no necessary connection between cause and effect.

readings.author: Frank Griffel
readings.title: Al-Ghazali (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
readings.note: Current, reliable orientation to his life and the causation argument.

readings.author: Frank Griffel
readings.title: Al-Ghazālī's Philosophical Theology
readings.note: Dismantles the 'al-Ghazālī ended Islamic science' caricature; reads his occasionalism with care.

concepts.gloss: Truth does not contradict truth, so demonstrated philosophy and revelation cannot really clash; where scripture's plain sense collides with a proof, the trained reader must take it as allegory — and the Law itself commands this. One truth reached two ways, for two kinds of mind, never two truths.

readings.author: Averroes
readings.title: Decisive Treatise & Epistle Dedicatory (Faṣl al-Maqāl, trans. Charles E. Butterworth)
readings.note: The argument that the Law obliges the philosopher to interpret, and that truth cannot contradict truth.

readings.author: Averroes
readings.title: The Incoherence of the Incoherence (Tahāfut al-Tahāfut, trans. Simon van den Bergh)
readings.note: The point-by-point rebuttal of al-Ghazālī, with extensive notes.

readings.author: Oliver Leaman
readings.title: Averroes and His Philosophy
readings.note: On Ibn Rushd's own program — distinct from the Latin 'Averroism' it inspired.

concepts.gloss: The excellent city mirrors the order of the cosmos: its ruler is a philosopher-prophet whose intellect touches the Active Intellect and whose imagination can cast demonstrated truth into images the many can live by. Plato's philosopher-king, recast where prophecy and reason deliver one wisdom.

readings.author: al-Fārābī
readings.title: The Political Writings (trans. Charles E. Butterworth)
readings.note: The practical-political treatises that complement the cosmological work.

readings.author: al-Fārābī
readings.title: On the Perfect State (Mabādiʾ Ārāʾ Ahl al-Madīna al-Fāḍila, trans. Richard Walzer)
readings.note: The standard critical edition of the virtuous-city cosmology and politics.

readings.author: Christopher A. Colmo
readings.title: Breaking with Athens: Alfarabi as Founder
readings.note: On the Plato/Aristotle synthesis and the philosopher-prophet.

concepts.gloss: In everything around us, what a thing is (its essence) is one question and that it is (its existence) another — existence is something added to essence. Follow the chain of such contingent beings and you reach one whose essence simply is to exist: the Necessary Being, owing its being to nothing outside itself.

readings.author: Avicenna
readings.title: The Metaphysics of The Healing (Kitāb al-Shifāʾ: Ilāhiyyāt, trans. Michael E. Marmura)
readings.note: The essence/existence distinction and the proof of the Necessary Existent (Bks I & VIII).
